what_it_does: Records a design breakdown for UI-feedback mode specifics, describing how a visible UI element should be mapped through a ui:// address to its rendering code:// location, dependencies, and editable source, and what inspect-mode tooling is needed.
observations: Frontmatter identifies the item as a current block in the dragnet-development channel, authored by ch-3mpkjg3r, and links it as part_of board://item-11da929d.
The title frames the note as a BD-J breakdown about UI-feedback mode: element identity → code.
It states that UI mode should carry the element ID, a description of the thing, its code location, and possibly dependencies, with the same granular-to-whole-page flexibility as content mode.
It proposes that UI elements already have stable ui:// addresses via data-ui-ref and can be resolved to code:// cards that capture imports/declares.
It describes a hierarchy: element < component < section < page, navigable by the same gesture.

It adds inspect-mode requirements: live element outline/highlight, resolved code location inside the envelope, jump-to-source, visual diff/preview before landing, and capture of computed state (text, props).
standouts: The element → ui:// → code:// → editable source + dependencies resolution chain.
Live outline/highlight inspect mode that surfaces element addresses to the annotation layer.
Visual diff/preview of a requested UI change before it lands.
Capture of the element's current computed state into the feedback envelope.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, channel, links, and history fields.
Body is markdown with labeled sections: SPECIFIED, IMPLIED, ADDITIONS.
Employs company addressing schemes: board://, ui://, and code://.
Links the item to a parent via a part_of relationship.
concerns: The acronym BD-J and the named stakeholder Tim are introduced without explanation in this file.
The inspect-mode behavior is described aspirationally but lacks trigger, activation, or implementation details.
No explicit acceptance criteria or schema for the 'envelope' are provided.
It assumes the running app can expose computed state and a visual diff/preview, but does not say how those are produced.
notes: A design note proposing that UI feedback annotations resolve elements through ui:// addresses into exact code:// source locations and include inspect-mode tooling such as outline, source jump, preview, and state capture.
questions: What does BD-J stand for in this context?
Who is Tim and what role does he play?
What is the exact syntax and semantics of a ui:// address and a code:// card?
How is inspect mode activated and what are its entry/exit conditions?
What is the precise structure of the 'envelope' mentioned in the content?
What is the granularity and behavior of the referenced 'content mode'?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is a breakdown of UI-feedback mode specifics as its title states.
apparent_themes: Traceability from visible UI to editable source
Stable addressing of elements and code artifacts
Reducing agent effort through direct edit targets
Actionable feedback with preview and state capture
intention_signals: It is trying to define a UI annotation/feedback mode that binds every visible element to its implementation source and dependencies, then equips the running app with inspection and preview affordances so a member can verify and edit the target directly.
novelty: It treats UI elements as ledger-addressable artifacts (ui://) and routes them through existing code-archaeology code:// cards to produce an editable source target, while also requiring live inspect outlines, source jump, and pre-land visual diff/preview.
contribution: The idea of a UI-to-code resolution path (element → ui:// → code:// → source + dependencies) plus a concrete set of inspect-mode requirements (live outline/highlight, source jump, visual diff/preview, computed-state capture) for UI feedback.
summary_for_embedding: This noticeboard item is a design breakdown for a UI-feedback mode that maps addressable UI elements (ui://) to their rendering code and dependencies (code://) and specifies inspect-mode affordances such as live outline, source jump, visual diff/preview, and computed-state capture.
intention_for_embedding: It was filed to define how UI-mode annotations should resolve visible elements into editable source targets and what tooling the running app must expose to support them.
